topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The feedback from the monitor was a jagged, ugly thing in my ears. I slammed my fist onto the mixing board, the cheap plastic protesting with a sharp crack. It was *my* melody, the one I’d poured three years of sleepless nights into, and now some amateur was butchering it. I watched the stage through the narrow gap in the curtain, my jaw tight. They were playing it with a sloppy, careless enthusiasm, their interpretation a cheap imitation of the raw feeling I’d captured. I didn't want them to succeed. I wanted them to stumble, to reveal the inadequacy of their grasp. I leaned back, letting a slow, cold burn settle in my chest. Every note they played was a direct insult to my vision.

[story 2]

I stood by the door, arms crossed so tightly my knuckles were white. The air in the venue was thick with stale beer and false applause. When the band finished their set, the silence felt heavy, loaded with the sound of their mediocre performance. Then, I heard it—a faint echo of my arrangement, distorted through the speakers. It was played by some up-and-comer, someone clearly trying too hard to sound profound. A tight, unpleasant knot formed just beneath my ribs. I walked toward the stage, not to congratulate them, but to stand near enough to watch their every strained movement. I made sure my expression was utterly neutral, a mask of detached observation, but inside, a corrosive resentment was bubbling up, hot and sharp.

[story 3]

My fingers hovered over the keys, paralyzed. The sound bleeding from the main PA system was undeniably mine—the intricate counter-rhythm, the specific harmonic shift that defined the track. It was being spun by someone else, someone whose interpretation felt both clumsy and offensively facile. A slow, deliberate tightening began in my shoulders. I didn't move to confront them; that would be too easy, too honest. Instead, I focused on the mechanics of the performance, cataloging every misplaced strum, every missed dynamic. A bitter taste coated my tongue. I took a slow, deliberate sip of water, letting the simmering irritation build into a solid, unmovable block of cold fury.
